finding: Limited-quantity promotions activate perceived competitive threat and physiological arousal, causing downstream generalized aggression even outside the competitive context. This effect manifests through hormone changes and occurs for luxury goods in resource-rich settings.

Increasing color saturation increases perceived size of objects through an arousal-attention pathway. High-saturation products are evaluated more favorably when size goals are large, and saturation affects perceived size of surrounding environments.

Products with polarizing (bimodal) rating distributions are perceived as more self-expressive than unimodal distributions. Consumers with low self-concept clarity prefer polarizing products despite more negative reviews because they signal unique identity.

Perceived financial well-being has two independent dimensions: current money management stress and expected future financial security. Both predict overall well-being comparably to job satisfaction and health; stress effects are mitigated at higher income levels.

Joint consumption choices balance self and others' preferences differently by self-construal and group size: interdependents balance regardless of group size, but independents balance only for small groups. Differential attention to others mediates this interaction.

Low self-concept clarity increases subscription retention for identity-relevant services by motivating avoidance of identity-change signals. The same low clarity decreases acquisition of new identity-relevant subscriptions, but affects neither retention nor acquisition of neutral services.
